title: Recognizing Depression in Older Adults (It's Not Just "Getting Older")
summary: Depression in older adults often looks different than it does in younger people, which is part of why it's so frequently missed. Here's what to actually look for.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Recognizing Depression in Older Adults (It's Not Just "Getting Older") | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Depression in older adults often looks different than it does in younger people, which is part of why it's so frequently missed. Here's what to actually lo
whatYouNeedToKnow: Sadness is often *not* the most visible sign — withdrawal, irritability, or physical complaints often show up first.
It's common (and wrong) to assume low mood is just a normal part of aging.
Depression in older adults is very treatable once it's recognized.

body.heading: What it actually looks like
paragraphs: Instead of appearing visibly sad, many older adults show depression through withdrawal from activities they used to enjoy, increased irritability, trouble sleeping, or vague physical complaints — aches, fatigue, low appetite — that don't have a clear medical cause. A parent might describe themselves as "just tired" or "fine, really" while quietly stopping the hobbies, calls, or outings that used to matter to them.

body.heading: Why it gets missed
paragraphs: It's an easy assumption to make that low energy or reduced interest is simply part of getting older, especially alongside real physical health changes. That assumption is understandable, but it means depression in this age group often goes unaddressed for a long time. Grief, isolation, and chronic illness can also overlap with depression's symptoms, which makes it easy to write off as one of those other things instead.

body.heading: How to bring it up
paragraphs: A direct, caring question tends to work better than a general one — "You haven't wanted to go to your usual Tuesday lunch lately, is everything okay?" opens more of a door than "Are you doing alright?" It also helps to raise it as something worth checking with their doctor, rather than something to fix on your own: "Would you be open to mentioning this at your next appointment?"

nextSteps: Write down specific changes you've noticed, with rough timing — this is genuinely useful for a doctor.
Suggest a regular checkup rather than framing it as a "mental health conversation," if that feels like an easier entry point.
Take the Caregiver Stress Self-Check yourself — supporting someone through this is genuinely taxing, and your own wellbeing matters here too.
disclaimer: This article describes general patterns and isn't a diagnostic tool. If you're worried about a sudden change in mood, or any mention of hopelessness, please contact their doctor or a mental health professional promptly.
